text.title: Use Case Modeling — Core Concepts
text.summary: Use case components, actor types, relationship types, and quality checklist for enterprise UC modeling.

rule.statement: A use case must have all four structural elements: actor, use case goal, system boundary, and scenario paths.
rule.rationale: Without all four, the UC is either a feature description (missing actor goal) or an org chart (missing system boundary).

list.item: Actor — who or what interacts with the system (human, system, or time trigger).
Use Case — the specific goal the actor achieves through the system.
System — the boundary of what is being built or analyzed.
Scenario — a specific path through the use case (main, alternative, or exception).

rule.statement: Actor type must be one of {human, system, time}. Job titles are not valid actor names.
rule.rationale: Job titles collapse to org-chart labels and change; goal-bearing actors (Customer, PaymentGateway, NightlyBatch) survive system redesigns.

list.item: Primary actors — initiate use cases to achieve their own goals.
Secondary actors — support the system (databases, external services, schedulers).
External systems — other systems that the UC interacts with to complete a goal.

rule.statement: Use include for shared behavior that always executes; use extend for optional behavior that adds to a base UC at declared extension points.
rule.rationale: The directionality of extend is consistently inverted — extension points belong to the base UC; the extending UC references them. Inverting this breaks traceability.

list.item: Include — base UC always calls the included UC (e.g., Place Order includes Process Payment).
Extend — extending UC optionally adds behavior at a declared extension point (e.g., Apply Coupon extends Place Order).
Generalization — specialized UC inherits the structure of a parent UC (e.g., Pay with Credit Card generalizes Pay).

rule.statement: Every UC must pass all nine quality gates before it is baselined.
rule.rationale: Missing any gate — especially alternative flows and exception flows — produces happy-path-only specs that fail in production.

list.item: Use case name is Verb + Noun.
Clear primary actor identified with type in {human, system, time}.
Main flow has 5-9 steps; each step = one actor action + one system response.
At least one alternative flow documented.
At least one exception flow documented.
Postconditions defined for success and failure.
Reviewed and signed off by stakeholders.

antipattern.description: Feature as use case — naming a UC "Display Screen" or "Show Report".
antipattern.reason: Features describe system behavior, not actor goals. UCs must be named from the actor's perspective: what does the actor achieve?

antipattern.description: UI nouns in system response — "displays popup", "shows modal", "highlights button".
antipattern.reason: UI elements change across design iterations; UCs must use system-behavior verbs (validates, persists, emits, notifies) to remain stable.

antipattern.description: Main flow with more than 9 steps.
antipattern.reason: Oversized main flows bundle multiple actor goals. Split into separate UCs; each UC covers one goal.

antipattern.description: Omitting exception flows — only happy path documented.
antipattern.reason: Exceptions are where systems fail and users abandon. A UC without exception flows cannot drive test coverage or error-handling design.
